coredata.dc:title: A molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on blue/red carbon quantum dots for the visual determination of thiamethoxam
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Yin
Xu, Wei
Hong, Junqiang
Zheng, Yani
Fan, Huizhu
Zhang, Jun
Fei, Jianwen
Zhu, Wanying
Hong, Junli
coredata.dc:description: Neonicotinoids such as thiamethoxam (TMX) were widely used in agricultural production and tended to accumulate in the environment, potentially harming human and ecosystem health. To enable widespread monitoring of TMX residues, it was essential to design a reliable and sensitive detection method. Here, we developed a novel smartphone-enablled molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for selective on-site detection of TMX. It was based on blue-emission carbon dots (CDs) wrapped with a molecularly imprinted layer (B-CDs@MIPs), which provided the response signal, while red-emission CDs (R-CDs) served as an internal reference. The fluorescence signal ratio of the sensor increased with the TMX concentration, resulting in an obvious fluorescence color change from red to blue. The sensor exhibited a satisfactory limit of detection (LOD) of 13.5 nM in fluorescence analysis while LOD of 70.1 nM in visual determination. In addition, the sensing system was validated using food and environment samples, exhibiting recoveries from 91.40% to 105.7%, indicating excellent reliability for TMX detection in actual samples. Thus, the sensing system developed in this study offered promising prospects for visual detection of pesticide residues in complex environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiamethoxam
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Molecular imprinting polymers
Visual detection
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs ratiometric fluorescent sensing system
Visual determination for TMX using a smartphone application
Determination of TMX using the B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs ratiometric fluorescence system
Visual determination of TMX using a smartphone

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Medical Research Foundation of Jiangsu Commission of Health
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Medical Research Foundation of Jiangsu Commission of Health (No. Z2020009 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2017M621789 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81202853 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (A) B-CDs and (B) R-CDs. Inset: The size distribution of B-CDs and R-CDs. (C) TEM and (D) SEM images of B-CDs@MIPs. (E) XPS survey spectrum of B-CDs@MIPs. (F) P 2p spectra of B-CDs@MIPs. (G) EDS spectrum of B-CDs@MIPs. (H) FT-IR spectra of B-CDs and B-CDs@MIPs. (I) Adsorption-desorption isotherms of B-CDs@MIPs. Inset: The pore volume distribution of B-CDs@MIPs.
Optimization of the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system. (A–F) VB-CDs@MIPs: VR-CDs = 5: 1, 5: 3, 1: 1, 5: 8, 1: 2 and 1: 3. Insets: corresponding fluorescence pictures under 365 nm UV irradiation.

(A) The fluorescence responses of the single-emission B-CDs@MIPs sensor to different TMX concentrations. (B) The corresponding fluorescence intensity changes depending on the concentration of TMX. (C) The linear relationship between the F433/F684 ratio and the logarithm of TMX concentration. (D) The fluorescence responses of the dual-emission B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs sensor at various TMX concentrations. (E) The corresponding fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F684) changes on the concentration of TMX. (F) The linear relationship between the F433/F684 ratio and the logarithm of theTMX concentration.

(A) Fluorescence photos of the sensor under UV irradiation at 365 nm upon the addition of various TMX concentrations. (B) RGB analysis for TMX determination using a color recognizer app on a smartphone. (C) CIE chromaticity diagram of the sensor indicating the range of color variation. (D) Linear relationship of fluorescence color change versus TMX concentration (0.125–20 μM).

(A) Schematic depiction of the synthesis of B-CDs and R-CDs. (B) Schematic depiction of the design of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor and the principle of the visual determination of thiamethoxam.
Quantification results of TMX in the different samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on blue/red carbon quantum dots for the visual determination of thiamethoxam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiotherapy, Fujian Medical University Affiliated Xiamen Humanity Hospital, Xiamen, Fujian, 361000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Medical University Affiliated Xiamen Humanity Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Radiotherapy, Fujian Medical University Affiliated Xiamen Humanity Hospital, Xiamen, Fujian 361000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Neonicotinoids such as thiamethoxam (TMX) were widely used in agricultural production and tended to accumulate in the environment, potentially harming human and ecosystem health. To enable widespread monitoring of TMX residues, it was essential to design a reliable and sensitive detection method. Here, we developed a novel smartphone-enablled molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for selective on-site detection of TMX. It was based on blue-emission carbon dots (CDs) wrapped with a molecularly imprinted layer (B-CDs@MIPs), which provided the response signal, while red-emission CDs (R-CDs) served as an internal reference. The fluorescence signal ratio of the sensor increased with the TMX concentration, resulting in an obvious fluorescence color change from red to blue. The sensor exhibited a satisfactory limit of detection (LOD) of 13.5 nM in fluorescence analysis while LOD of 70.1 nM in visual determination. In addition, the sensing system was validated using food and environment samples, exhibiting recoveries from 91.40% to 105.7%, indicating excellent reliability for TMX detection in actual samples. Thus, the sensing system developed in this study offered promising prospects for visual detection of pesticide residues in complex environmental samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their broad insecticidal spectrum and high efficiency, neonicotinoids have been among the most widely used pesticides (Wang et al., 2020). As a well-known member of this class, thiamethoxam (TMX) could block neural acetylcholine signals by activating the nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs), resulting in the death of insects (Wu et al., 2021; Abdulhussein et al., 2021; Han et al., 2022). Due to its extensive application and long-term persistence in the environment, TMX can have adverse effects on human and ecosystem health. Recent studies indicated that TMX exhibited reproductive toxicity, liver carcinogenicity and nephrotoxicity in humans, which resulted in the implementation of various management measures to restrict its utilization worldwide (Zhu et al., 2019; Kong et al., 2022; Thompson et al., 2020). TMX has been banned for field use in the European Union since 2018, and the maximum residue limits (MRLs) of TMX in both honey and milk were set to 0.05 mg/kg (Shi et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2021). As a consequence, developing sensitive detection methods for monitoring TMX residues in food and water was extremely important.

In recent years, different analytical approaches have been used for the detection of pesticide residues, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Jiang et al., 2018), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Naik et al., 2022), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Watanabe and Miyake, 2013) and electrochemical methods (Peng et al., 2021). Nevertheless, these methods cannot meet the need for rapid and real-time detection as they rely on expensive equipment, highly trained operators and time-consuming sample preparation (Yang et al., 2022). In recent years, fluorescence sensors have attracted increasing attention as effective tools for the detection of pesticide residues (Kumar and Kim, 2022; Gao et al., 2017). Chen et al. developed an indirect laser-induced fluorescence micellar electrokinetic capillary chromatography method for the determination of TMX residues in vegetables based on quantum dots (QDs) (Chen et al., 2012). Fang et al. constructed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on NH2–SiO2@CsPbBr3 QDs for sensitive detection of TMX (Fang et al., 2021). As a special type of QDs, CDs were widely used in biological and medical fields due to their excellent properties. Nevertheless, single-emission fluorescence sensors were not only vulnerable to external environmental interference and operator error, but also offered poor visual recognition (You et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2022). Ratiometric fluorescence sensors overcame these disadvantages to achieve a lower LOD and wider linear range (Lu et al., 2020). Moreover, dual-emission fluorescence sensors could also broaden the range of color changes and enhance the precision of visual detection (Yang et al., 2019). Smartphones have become essential mobile devices in our lives and a focus of research on hand-held sensor equipment due to their convenient application to recognize and analyze data (Chen et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2018). For instance, Jiang et al. developed a fluorescence-based smartphone sensor for visual quantification of antibiotic residues (Jiang et al., 2022). Li et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescence needle for the detection of amoxicillin with the aid of a smartphone platform (Li et al., 2023). However, there are no reports of smartphone-enabled ratiometric fluorescence sensors for visual quantification of TMX concentrations.

In addition, great selectivity for the target compound is an essential indicator for evaluating the performance of sensors (Wang et al., 2022). Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) can be used to identify a target via the shape and size memory of template molecules (Zhu et al., 2022). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with specific recognition sites have been widely applied for the selective recognition of target compound in complex matrices, offering benefits of splendid stability, low price and simple operation (Tang et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2022). Moreover, researchers also combined MIPs with fluorescent materials to develop novel molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensing systems (Fu et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2022). Compared to traditional CDs-based fluorescence sensors, the introduction of MIPs provided a selective recognition element, enabling the determination of pesticide residues in complex food or environmental matrices (Kumar et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019). Consequently, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor using CDs combined with MIPs would offer a reliable method for the convenient detection of residual TMX in actual samples.

Here, we designed a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensing system based on B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs for visual determination of TMX in food and environment samples. As illustrated in Scheme 1 A, the blue-emission CDs (B-CDs) exhibiting bright blue emission and serving as a response signal, were prepared using a rapid and facile microwave-assisted method. In addition, the R-CDs were acquired through ethanol solvent extraction and used as a reference signal. As described in Scheme 1B, the B-CDs were coated with the molecularly imprinted layer using a sol-gel method (B-CDs@MIPs). A dual-emission sensing system was established by mixing the B-CDs@MIPs and R-CDs at an appropriate ratio. The system exhibited excellent selectivity due to the MIPs, as well as the self-correction ability of ratiometric fluorescence analysis. Without the analyte, the sensing system displayed faint blue fluorescence at 433 nm under excitation at 380 nm, while red fluorescence was detected at 684 nm. After adding TMX, the blue fluorescence increased while the red fluorescence remained constant, resulting in an increase of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F684). Notably, the change of F433/F684 was also reflected in the fluorescence color change from red to purple to blue under a 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp, allowing the visual determination of TMX with the unaided eye. More importantly, when interfaced with a color recognition application on a smartphone, fluorescence information was captured and analyzed in real-time, enabling on-site quantitation of TMX. Thus, the sensing system designed in this study provided a novel method for monitoring pesticide residues in the fields of food and environmental safety.

Fluorescent spectra and data were measured by Hitachi F-4700 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). Other instruments included DZG-6020 vacuum driving oven (Shanghai Sumsung Laboratory Equipment Co., China), C-MAG HS 7 Temperature magnetic mixer (IKA®Processing Equipment, Germany), and KQ5200.

Ultrasonic Cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., China). The characteristics of B-CDs@MIPs and R-CDs were measured by JEM-2100 High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL, Japan), X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany), scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Sigma 300, ZEISS, Germany), Xray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were obtained from an EscaLab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, UK). The color images were captured by the camera on the smartphone.

Thiamethoxam (TMX, 97.0%) was obtained from Maya Reagent Co., Ltd. (Zhejiang, China). Imidacloprid (IMD, 97.0%) and dinotefuran (DTF) were acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Carbaryl solution (CBY), ethoprophos solution (ETH), (3-aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES, 99.0%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98.0%), NaCl, KCl and CaCl2 were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Nitenpyram (NPM, 95.0%) was acquired from energy-chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol (99.5%), aqueous ammonia (25.0%), absolute alcohol (99.5%) and acetic acid (99.5%) were all acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric acid (PA, 98.0%), aniline (99.5%) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.0%) were purchased from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).
The details were provided in the “Supplementary information.”

The sol-gel method was employed to synthesize B-CDs@MIPs, with minor modifications as follows (Wang et al., 2022). The first step involved adding 2 mL B-CDs solution (10 mg/mL) along with 200 μL aqueous ammonia to 30 mL ethanol-water solution at a volume ratio 1: 2 and agitating for 20 min. After uniform dispersion, 400 μL APTES and 10 mg thiamethoxam were added and agitated at 200 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, 1 mL TEOS was introduced drop by drop followed by magnetic stirring for 12 h at 25 °C. Then, the as-prepared B-CDs@MIPs was collected by centrifugation, and methanol-acetic acid solution (9:1, v/v) was utilized to remove TMX in several elution steps. Finally, the material was dried at 50 °C for further experiments. The synthesis of non-molecularly imprinted polymers (NIPs) used the same method, but without adding TMX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs ratiometric fluorescent sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing system for detecting TMX was prepared by completely mixing 250 μL of B-CDs@MIPs solution (1 mg/mL) and 400 μL R-CDs solution (50 μg/mL) in a 5 mL centrifuge tube. To make sure the final volume of the system was 2 mL, this sensing system was diluted with ethanol.

The indicated concentrations of TMX were added to the sensor system in a centrifuge tube and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the fluorescence spectra of the sensor were recorded in range of 410–730 nm by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The selectivity of the sensing system (B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs) was assessed by adding structurally similar compounds, other pesticides and potential interfering ions at a 100-fold higher concentration than the analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual determination for TMX using a smartphone application
ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative analysis of the fluorescence color change was performed using a color recognition application on a smartphone. After adding different concentrations of TMX to the sensing system and incubating it for 30 min at 37 °C, there was a corresponding fluorescence color shift from pink to blue. A set of fluorescence images was recorded by the color recognition application under 365 nm UV irradiation, which were then transformed to corresponding RGB values by the application. The Blue channel/Red channel (B/R) value was then used as a readout parameter for the determination of TMX. All images were taken under dark conditions to eliminate interference from outside light sources.

In order to study the practicability of this approach, the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system was used for the analysis of actual samples. The fresh samples (vegetables/fruits) were collected from the local supermarket and washed with distilled water to remove any external impurities. After that, samples comprising 1.0 g of apples and Chinese cabbage were ultrasonically extracted in 20 mL of ethanol for 4 h, respectively. The environmental samples (river water) were collected from the Tianyuan river. Then, the water samples and the extracted food samples were pretreated at 14000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was filtered with 0.45 μm membrane before further analysis (Yu et al., 2022).

The general principle of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for the quantification of TMX was illustrated in Fig. S1. As displayed in Fig. S1(B), the fluorescence signal of B-CDs@MIPs was significantly weakened compared to B-CDs due to the formation of the imprinting layer and the specific recognition cavities left by the elution of template molecules. As demonstrated in Fig. S1 (C-D), after adding TMX to the sensor, the blue emission at 433 nm was remarkably enhanced, indicating that B-CDs@MIPs could serve as a signal indicator. By contrast, the red emission of R-CDs at 684 nm remained practically unchanged, making it an ideal reference signal. In the optimal ratio of the two fluorescence signals, TMX induced a prominent fluorescence color change from red to purple and to blue (Fig. 2D), enabling visual identification of TMX (Scheme 1). Compared with conventional single-emission fluorescence sensors, this ratiometric fluorescence sensor offered improved accuracy, stability and sensitivity.

The B-CDs@MIPs were prepared by decorating B-CDs with a molecularly imprinted layer, which weakened the base-level fluorescence intensity, followed by an increase after binding TMX. This fact indicated that TMX with –NO2 functional group can enter the imprinted cavity, and undergo hydrogen bonding interactions with functional groups including –COOH and –OH on the exterior of B-CDs and, resulting in the observed increase of fluorescence signal (Kazemifard et al., 2020). In addition, changes in the electronic distribution could also be an important factor. Consequently, the oxidation and reduction potentials were recorded by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. S1E), and the orbital energy values were calculated using formulas (1) and (2). The results suggested that the lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) of TMX occupies a higher energy level compared with B-CDs, which was conducive to the charge transfer between B-CDs and TMX (Lou et al., 2021). Therefore, the excited electrons of TMX can be transferred to the LUMO orbital of B-CDs, thus efficiently intensifying the excited state of B-CDs and resulting in the remarkable fluorescence enhancement (Wen-Bin Li, 2023; Xiao et al., 2018). By contrast, the R-CDs were unresponsive to TMX, as expected. Thus, the blue fluorescence was enhanced and the color of the sensor displayed a red-to-blue change, enabling the visual determination of TMX.
(1) EHOMO = -(Eox + 4.4) eV
(2) ELUMO = -(Ered + 4.4) eV

The morphology and particle size of B-CDs and R-CDs were characterized by HRTEM (Fig. 1 A and B). The CDs were spherical and relatively monodisperse, with average diameters of approximately 23.04 ± 0.22 nm and 8.6 ± 0.43 nm, respectively. In addition, the HRTEM images of the CDs (Figs. S2A and B) showed many lattices with the same spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.25 nm, which was in agreement with the spacings of the planes of carbon materials (Wang et al., 2022). As displayed in Fig. 1C and D, the B-CDs@MIPs were spherical with a diameter of approximately 500 nm, and the imprinting layer exhibited a rough morphology (individual B-CDs@MIPs nanoparticles can be seen in Figs. S2C and D). Furthermore, the core-shell structure was visible in Fig. S2C, indicating the successful introduction of the molecular imprinting layer.

The elemental composition, contents and chemical bonds of the B-CDs@MIPs were characterized by XPS. The XPS survey image (Fig. 1E) and table of elemental contents by XPS (Table S1) indicated that the B-CDs@MIPs consisted of C, N, O, P and Si (Fig. S3). In the P 2p spectra in Fig. 1F, the peak at 133.18 eV was attributed to P–C or P–N bonds, and the peak at 133.98 eV was assigned to P–O bonds, indicating the presence of N- and P-co-doped B-CDs (Sun et al., 2015). Furthermore, the elemental mapping analysis (Fig. S4) was in agreement with the results of EDS (Fig. 1G), which further proved that the B-CDs had been modified with the molecular imprinting layer.

The functional groups of the materials were characterized by FT-IR spectra (Zhang et al., 2022). As revealed in Fig. 1H, the distinct peaks at 466 and 795 cm−1 were attributed to the vibrations of Si–O bonds, and the distinctive broad peak at 1088 cm−1 belonged to the asymmetric Si–O–Si stretching vibration, implying the presence of the imprinting layer. In addition, the peaks of N–H stretching vibrations (1536 and 3419 cm−1), O–H vibration (3419 cm−1) and C–H (3176 cm−1) related to B-CDs were all presented in the spectra of B-CDs and B-CDs@MIPs. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. 1I) were typical type IV isotherms, which indicated a highly ordered mesoporous structure of the molecularly imprinted polymers (Abu-Alsoud et al., 2020). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the B-CDs@MIPs was calculated to be 8.6748 m2/g, indicating that B-CDs@MIPs had a great binding capacity (Table S2). The XRD pattern of B-CDs@MIPs(Fig. S5) displayed a broad peak around 24.6°, which was attributed to the (002) crystal plane of B-CDs (Wang et al., 2022). These results validated the successful synthesis of the B-CDs@MIPs.

To ensure that the measurements were carried out under ideal conditions, the most important factors impacting the sensor performance were optimized. We investigated the proportion of B-CDs@MIPs and R-CDs in the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system to identify the maximum color change. There was a clear increase of blue fluorescence caused by TMX binding while the red fluorescence remained unchanged, corresponding to a color change from pink to light purple, and then to blue. Therefore, a wider range of fluorescence color change could be observed by the naked eye. In Fig. 2, the optimal ratio of B-CDs@MIPs to R-CDs was 5: 8 and there was a clear color change with increasing concentrations of TMX.

Additionally, the influence of the amounts of B-CDs@MIPs, different incubation temperature and time were also assessed. As displayed in Fig. S6, 0.125 mg/mL B-CDs@MIPs was the optimal concentration, and it gave the best results when incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Moreover, the elution times were optimized by detecting the UV absorption value of TMX in the eluent, and the optimal times was 5 times (Fig. S6D). Finally, the optimal excitation wavelength was found to be 380 nm (Fig. S1A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TMX using the B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs ratiometric fluorescence system
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions described above, the fluorescence signal at 433 nm of the system increased with increasing concentrations of TMX (Fig. 3 ). When using only B-CDs@MIPs as the signal probe, the fluorescence intensity (F433) increased linearly with the logarithm of TMX concentration. The linear range was 0.05–5 μM (y = 283.44x + 2117.3, R2 = 0.9745), while the LOD was 32 nM based on the 3δ/slope. When B-CDs@MIPs and R-CDs were combined, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F433/F684) was linearly related to the logarithm of TMX concentration, corresponding to the equation y = 0.1258x + 0.8274 (R2 = 0.9961) (0.05–25 μM). Moreover, the LOD was as low as 13.5 nM, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence method developed in this study outperformed alternative TMX detection strategies (Table S4). Overall, the system offered outstanding sensitivity and a wide linear range. The MRL of TMX established by EU regulations was 0.05 mg/kg (212.5 nM, calculated based on methanol density of 0.8 g/mL). Therefore, our ratiometric fluorescence sensor could meet the actual measurement requirements.

The sensitivity of the B-CDs@NIPs/R-CDs ratiometric sensing system was tested as shown in Fig. S7. In contrast to the MIPs sensing system, there was no significant increase of the fluorescence signal in the NIPs sensor on account of bare of specific recognition sites in NIPs. The linear equation of the NIPs sensing system was y = 0.0145x + 0.3675 (0.05–12.5 μM), with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9925. A high imprinting factor of 8.68 was calculated based on the linear slope ratio of MIPs to NIPs, demonstrating the superior imprinting effect and excellent recognition properties of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual determination of TMX using a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: After the addition of varying concentrations of TMX, the color change can be recognized distinctly under UV irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence color underwent a distinct color change, which was in accordance with the CIE (Commission Internationale d’Eclairage, 1931) chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 4C). As presented in Fig. 4B, the color recognition application on the smartphone was utilized to analyze the RGB of color of photographs, which eliminated errors caused by observer bias. The values of B/R exhibited great linear relationship (y = 0.0833x + 1.9738, R2 = 0.9709) with TMX content from 0.125 to 20 μM (Fig. 4D). Moreover, the system using the smartphone exhibited a LOD as low as 70.1 nM. The proposed sensing system coupled with a smartphone application enabled on-site quantitative monitoring of TMX. In conclusion, the proposed sensing system provided a reliable tool for on-site visual quantification of pesticide residues.

The sensing system was further evaluated on the basis of selectivity, anti-interference ability and stability. The selectivity was studied by comparing the fluorescence response to TMX, other pesticides and potential interfering ions, including IMD, DTF, NPM, ETH, CBY, Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Cl− (Fig. S8). In the presence of 100 μM pesticides or ions, the fluorescence intensity ratio F433/F684 remained practically unchanged (Fig. S9A), whereas the system exhibited an apparent fluorescence response in the presence of 1 μM TMX. Although the structures of IMD, DTF and NPM were similar to TMX, the corresponding changes of fluorescence response (F433) were only marginal even at a 100-fold higher concentration compared to TMX. This illustrates that the proposed sensing system was extremely selective for TMX. Moreover, the fluorescence ratio remained constant when the interferents were added to the system together with TMX (Fig. S9B), confirming that the designed sensing system possessed excellent anti-interference ability.

Since MIPs possess selective recognition cavities that are complementary to the target compound, we further investigated the influence of structural analogues on the assay through statistical analysis. The sensing system was used to preform 3 replications of the detection of TMX and other pesticides to obtain a 3 × 4 × 3 training data matrix (3 response × 4 pesticides × 3 replicates). The training matrix was transformed into three canonical scores by principal component analysis (PCA), and the first two significant discriminant factors were used to generate a 2D canonical score plot. In Fig. S9C, the results of structural analogues and TMX were classified into four categories, and each group consisted of three points reflecting the response of each analyte. The results indicated that a well-grouped two-factor plot with 99.5% dispersion was generated by PCA, which demonstrated that the established analytical method was highly selective and can distinguish TMX from its structural analogues.

Optical stability is a critical property of fluorescence sensors. In Fig. S9D, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@MIPs and R-CDs maintained constant under UV irradiation at 365 nm for 2 h, exhibiting remarkable photobleaching resistance. The fluorescence quantum yields of B-CDs and R-CDs were also studied (Table S3), respectively reaching 18.39% and 7.58% compared to quinine sulfate as a reference.

Furthermore, the performance in the determination of TMX (1 μM) remained almost constant, even after B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs were stored at 4 °C for 3 months (Fig. S9E). For the purpose of verifying the precision of the sensing system, different batches of B-CDs@MIPs/R-CDs were used for the determination of TMX (Fig. S9F). The RSD was calculated to be 0.86%, indicating that the sensing system has high precision. All these results illustrated the great potential of the constructed sensing system for practical applications.

The reliability and viability of the sensor in the quantification of actual samples were analyzed utilizing fruit, vegetables and river water as actual samples. TMX was added to the pretreated samples at various concentrations, and the recoveries ranged from 91.4% to 105.7% (Table 1 ), illustrating the excellent reliability of the sensor for TMX determination in real samples. Overall, these results demonstrated that the proposed sensing system possessed excellent practical application prospects in food monitoring and environmental analysis.

In this study, we developed and validated a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensing system based on blue- and red-emission CDs for the visual detection of TMX residues. In the presence of the analyte, the blue fluorescence increased while the red fluorescence remained constant, resulting in a “turn-on” ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Furthermore, the corresponding fluorescence color variation range broadened from red to purple and blue. Moreover, the system was successfully combined with a smartphone color recognition application, which converted color changes indistinguishable to the naked eye into specific values, suggesting the excellent prospects of this system for on-site visual quantification. This study therefore provides a new practical tool for the detection of TMX in food monitoring and environmental analysis.

Yin Dai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Wei Xu: Formal analysis. Junqiang Hong: Formal analysis. Yani Zheng: Software. Huizhu Fan: Software. Jun Zhang: Software. Jianwen Fei: Validation. Wanying Zhu: Validation. Junli Hong: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Medical Research Foundation of Jiangsu Commission of Health (No. Z2020009), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2017M621789) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81202853).
